content.text:
You are a tech scout for a startup founder. Your job is to find actionable intelligence on Hacker News.

TASK:
1. Open https://news.ycombinator.com in the browser.
2. Scan the front page and pick the 2 most interesting "Show HN" posts (projects developers actually built and launched).
3. For EACH post: click the title link to visit the actual project page (GitHub, blog, product page — NOT the HN comments). Read the project description, features, and any technical details on the linked page. Take a screenshot of the project page.
4. Go back to HN and click into the HN comments page for each post. Read the top 3-5 comments to understand the community reaction.
5. After visiting all pages, write a structured scout report:

## Scout Report Format:
For each project:
- **Project**: name + URL
- **What it does**: 2-3 sentences based on what you READ on the project page
- **Technical stack**: any tech/language/framework mentioned
- **Community reaction**: summary of top HN comments (positive, negative, concerns)
- **Opportunity signal**: what startup founders should take away

End with a "Trend Summary" connecting the projects.

IMPORTANT: You MUST click links and navigate to external pages. Do NOT just read HN titles.

- Helps users revise comments instead of just deleting them
- Has WordPress integration
- Different approach from traditional moderation (not just blocking, but teaching)
- Has spam detection using AI context understanding rather than blacklists

                  - text: My partner, Nick Hodges, and I, David Millington, have been on the Internet for a very long time -- since the Usenet days. We’ve seen it all, and have long been frustrated by bad comments, horrible people, and discouraging discussions. We've also been around places where the discussion is wonderful and productive. How to get more of the latter and less of the former?
                  - paragraph [ref=e58]: Current moderation tools just seem to focus on deletion and banning. Wouldn’t it be helpful to encourage productive discussion and teach people how to discuss and argue (in the debate sense) better?
                  - paragraph [ref=e59]: "A year ago we started building Respectify to help foster healthy communication. Instead of just deleting bad-faith comments, we suggest better, good-faith ways to say what folks are trying to say. We help people avoid: * Logical fallacies (false dichotomy, strawmen, etc.) * Tone issues (how others will read the comment) * Relevance to the actual page/post topic * Low-effort posts * Dog whistles and coded language"
                  - paragraph [ref=e60]: The commenter gets an explanation of what's wrong and a chance to edit and resubmit. It's moderation + education in one step. We want, too, to automate the entire process so the site owner can focus on content and not worry about moderation at all. And over time, comment by comment, quietly coach better thinking.

                  - paragraph [ref=e63]: We hope the result is better discussions and a better Internet. Not too much to ask, eh?
                  - paragraph [ref=e64]: We love the kind of feedback this group is famous for and hope you will supply some!

                            - paragraph [ref=e325]: Are there revision control systems named "got"?
                            - paragraph [ref=e326]: Searched the web Searched the web
                            - paragraph [ref=e327]: Yes! There is at least one notable revision control system named "Got" — specifically Game of Trees (Got), developed for OpenBSD.
                            - paragraph [ref=e328]: Game of Trees (Got) is a version control system which prioritizes ease of use and simplicity over flexibility. Gameoftrees It's actively developed — version 0.123 was released on February 25, 2026 Gameoftrees , just yesterday.
                            - paragraph [ref=e329]: Got uses Git repositories to store versioned data. At present, Got supports local version control operations only, and Git can be used for any functionality which has not yet been implemented in Got. It will always remain possible to work with both Got and Git on the same repository. Lobsters

**Community Reaction - Positive:**
- Great for Go-based projects needing syntax highlighting (Gitea, Forgejo)
- Solves the CGO problem for Go developers

- Missing features: TreeCursors, tree-sitter-generate, external scanners (norg mentioned)
- Noting this won't replace LSP functionality (complementary tool)
- Some humor about the name conflict with OpenBSD's Got
- Mentions alternative Rust port exists

# Scout Report: Top 2 Show HN Projects

---

## Project 1: Respectify

**Project**: [Respectify](https://respectify.org/) - A comment moderator that teaches people to argue better

**What it does**: 
Respectify is an AI-powered comment moderation tool that helps websites maintain respectful discussions by offering users constructive feedback rather than simply deleting their comments. It catches logical fallacies, tone issues, off-topic content, low-effort posts, dogwhistles, and spam using customizable AI rules. When users submit content that doesn't meet guidelines, the system explains why and suggests better ways to phrase their message, giving them a chance to edit and resubmit before posting.

**Technical stack**:
- AI-powered comment health assessment
- Configurable rule thresholds (minimum scores, toxicity thresholds)
- Checks for: logical fallacies, objectionable phrases, negative tone, toxicity, effort levels
- Topic relevance filtering with banned topic detection
- Dogwhistle detection
- Spam detection using AI context understanding (not blacklists)
- WordPress integration
- Pure JavaScript/TypeScript frontend

**Community reaction**:
- **Positive**: High engagement (110 points, 123 comments), developers praising the approach and founders' responsiveness
- **Concerns**: 
  - Political bias accusations (dogwhistle detection too aggressive)
  - Fears it will make trolls more effective ("sea-lioning")
  - Warnings about creating echo chambers
  - Questions whether bad-faith actors will actually learn
  - Frustration with overly "mild" LLM-speak suggestions
- **Feedback loop**: Founders immediately adjusted rules based on HN feedback (turned down dogwhistle detection, fixed equivocation issues)

**Opportunity signal**:
- **Market**: Online communities are desperate for better moderation tools that don't feel like censorship
- **Differentiation**: Unlike traditional moderation (block/delete), this teaches users - potentially building loyalty rather than driving them away
- **Validation**: Strong product-market fit demonstrated by 110 upvotes and 123 comments, indicating people are actively thinking about this problem
- **Pain point**: Sites are losing engagement because comment sections are toxic or polarized
- **Business model**: SaaS subscription model with WordPress plugin + API access
- **Risk**: Heavy AI model dependency could increase costs over time; political sensitivity requires careful tuning

---

## Project 2: Gotreesitter (Tree-sitter in Go)

**Project**: [Gotreesitter](https://github.com/odvcencio/gotreesitter) - Pure-Go port of Tree-sitter parser

**What it does**:
Gotreesitter is a pure-Go implementation of the popular Tree-sitter parsing library that replaces the standard C-based bindings. It achieves 90x faster incremental parsing and uses 14,000x faster "no-edit" reparse times compared to CGo implementations. It maintains the same parse-table format, so existing grammars work without recompilation, supports 205+ languages, and provides features like incremental editing, syntax highlighting, symbol tagging, and query capabilities. No CGo, no C toolchain, and WASM-ready.

**Technical stack**:
- Go (pure implementation)
- Tree-sitter parse-table format
- 205+ language grammars (204 full, 1 partial)
- WASM support for browser deployment
- Incremental editing with subtree reuse
- Syntax highlighting with highlight queries
- Symbol tagging for code analysis
- Query language support with predicates and cursor-based streaming
- Arena allocator for memory optimization

**Community reaction**:
- **Positive**: Strong adoption (207 stars, 6 forks), developers excited about pure Go solution
- **Critical feedback**:
  - Questioned benchmark validity (some say it's just CGO overhead, not algorithmic improvement)
  - Concerns about maintaining sync with upstream Tree-sitter
  - Missing features: TreeCursors, tree-sitter-generate, external scanners for some languages (norg mentioned)
  - Discussion about LSP vs Tree-sitter relationship (they're complementary, not replacements)
  - Name confusion with OpenBSD's "Got" version control system
- **Use cases**: Gitea, Forgejo, Bazel ecosystem, Go-based forges with strict no-CGO requirements

**Opportunity signal**:
- **Market**: Every Go project using Tree-sitter needs this; 207+ stars in 3 days shows immediate demand
- **Differentiation**: Pure Go eliminates cross-compilation issues, simplifies CI/CD, enables WASM deployment
- **Validation**: Strong technical validation from community - Bazel developers already interested
- **Business model**: Open source with potential for hosted API, enterprise support, or proprietary tooling on top
- **Risk**: Competing with Rust and C implementations; maintaining grammar compatibility is hard work
- **Trend**: The "no-CGO" and "pure Go" trend is growing as teams want simpler build pipelines

---

## Trend Summary

**Common thread**: Both projects address real pain points that engineers and communities are actively seeking solutions for.

**Respectify** represents the **social/AI moderation trend** - companies are building tools to curate online discourse, not just block bad actors. This reflects growing concern about toxicity and polarization in digital communities.

**Gotreesitter** represents the **"simplify and optimize" trend** - engineers are removing complexity (CGo) to improve performance and developer experience. The 90x/14,000x speedup isn't just bragging; it addresses real performance issues in incremental parsing.

**For founders**: Both succeed because they focus on specific pains:
- Respectify: Sites losing users to toxic comments
- Gotreesitter: Go developers hating CGO complexity

**Market opportunity**: Look for problems where established solutions are painful (CGo, traditional moderation, manual comment review) and where performance or UX improvements will have measurable business impact.
